coredata.dc:title: Selective homocysteine detection of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots: Synergistic effect of surface catalysis and photoluminescence sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Zou, Yajie
Feng, Xiaoqiang
Zhao, Yun
Wei, Zhiheng
Zhu, Wei
Zhao, Menghan
Wang, Ting
Liu, Zhiduo
Tang, Shiwei
Wang, Gang
Guo, Qinglei
Chen, Da
coredata.dc:description: As a new photoluminescence functional material, graphene quantum dots have aroused widely entertaining. However, the complicated surficial chemistry micro-circumstance of graphene quantum dots hinders the further study of graphene quantum dots. In this paper, we demonstrate that the selectivity and sensitivity photoluminescence (PL) detection of homocysteine based on the synergistic effect of surface catalysis and photoluminescence sensing for nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs). Due to the localized superacid microenvironment of N-GQDs, the homocysteine cyclized into homocysteine thiolactone efficiently and selectively. Meanwhile, the aromatic N in N-GQDs adsorbs homocysteine thiolactone selectively. The following static quenching results in the outstanding selectivity and sensitivity PL quenching of N-GQDs with the presence of homocysteine. The detection limit is 5 × 10−11 M, which is much lower than most reported detection methods. We also demonstrate that N-GQDs are able to display a PL response to homocysteine in the living cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Photoluminescence detection
Localized superacid microenvironment
Catalytic sensor
Homocysteine

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image (b) size distribution histogram, (c) HR-TEM, (d) AFM image of N-GQDs. (e) Raman spectrum of N-GQDs.
(a) XPS survey spectrum of N-GQDs. The inset shows the surficial chemistry micro-circumstance of N-GQDs. (b) C-1 s, (c) O-1 s and (d) N-1 s XPS spectra of N-GQDs.

(a) UV–vis, (b) PL and PLE, (c) PL decay curve of N-GQDs aqueous solution (0.1 mg mL−1). (d) PL spectra of N-GQDs aqueous solution with different [Hom] ([Hom] = 0-50 nM, the concentration of N-GQDs aqueous solution is 0.1 mg mL−1). (e) A linear relation between F0/F of N-GQDs aqueous solution and [Hom]. F and F0 is the PL intensity of N-GQDs aqueous solution with and without homocysteine, respectively. (f) The PL intensity change of N-GQDs with the presence of different ions (Na+, K+, Mg2+, Al3+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Ag+, Pb2+, Hg2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, SO4 2- and Cl−). The concentration of N-GQDs aqueous solution is 0.1 mg mL−1, the concentration of ions is 0.1 M. F and F0 is the PL intensity of N-GQDs aqueous solution with and without the presence of ions, respectively. (g) The PL intensity change of N-GQDs with the presence of different amino acids (lysine, threonine, phenylalanine, cysteine, homocysteine, valine, methionine, tryptophan, glutamine, proline). The concentration of N-GQDs aqueous solution is 0.1 mg mL−1, the concentration of amino acids is 10 mM. F and F0 is the PL intensity of N-GQDs aqueous solution with and without the presence of amino acids, respectively.

(a) The schematic diagram for the cyclization reaction of homocysteine and the structure of cysteine. (b) the ΔG of surface catalytic cyclization reaction for the homocysteine thiolactone on N-GQDs with different oxygen-containing functional groups contents. (c) The content of homocysteine thiolactone when 5 mL, 1 mM homocysteine aqueous solution, and 5 mL, 1 mg mL−1 N-GQDs aqueous solution were mixed and stirred for 10 min under 25 °C. (d) ΔG for the adsorption process of homocysteine thiolactone when the N atoms in N-GQDs is aromatic N, NH2 and CNHC, respectively. (e) ΔG for the adsorption process of different amino acids (lysine, threonine, phenylalanine, cysteine, homocysteine, valine, methionine, tryptophan, glutamine, proline) or homocysteine thiolactone of N-GQDs. (f) PL intensity change of NH-GQDs (0.1 mg mL−1) or NH2-GQDs (0.1 mg mL−1) with the presence of homocysteine (50 nM). F and F0 is the PL intensity of NH-GQDs or NH2-GQDs aqueous solution with and without homocysteine, respectively. (g) the change of τ of N-GQDs (0.1 mg mL−1) with different [Hom]. τ and τ0 is the PL lifetime of N-GQDs aqueous solution with and without homocysteine, respectively.

(a) Metabolic activity of rADSCs cells with different concentrations of N-GQDs (0–600 μg mL−1). (b) Confocal fluorescence microphotograph of rADSCs (treated by 0.5 mg mL−1 N-GQDs aqueous solution). (c) Confocal fluorescence microphotograph of rADSCs treated by 0.5 mg mL−1 N-GQDs aqueous solution and 20 nM homocysteine aqueous solution.
A summary of the detection limit and linear range of the reported detection methods for homocysteine (NA: not mentioned).

head.ce:title: Selective homocysteine detection of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots: Synergistic effect of surface catalysis and photoluminescence sensing

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As a new photoluminescence functional material, graphene quantum dots have aroused widely entertaining. However, the complicated surficial chemistry micro-circumstance of graphene quantum dots hinders the further study of graphene quantum dots. In this paper, we demonstrate that the selectivity and sensitivity photoluminescence (PL) detection of homocysteine based on the synergistic effect of surface catalysis and photoluminescence sensing for nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs). Due to the localized superacid microenvironment of N-GQDs, the homocysteine cyclized into homocysteine thiolactone efficiently and selectively. Meanwhile, the aromatic N in N-GQDs adsorbs homocysteine thiolactone selectively. The following static quenching results in the outstanding selectivity and sensitivity PL quenching of N-GQDs with the presence of homocysteine. The detection limit is 5 × 10−11 M, which is much lower than most reported detection methods. We also demonstrate that N-GQDs are able to display a PL response to homocysteine in the living cells.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), which are zero-dimensional graphene derivative, have aroused widely interesting since the first reported in 2007 [1]. Due to the unique optical properties [2], interfacial properties [3], semiconductor properties [1] and catalytic performances [4], GQDs have been widely used in bio-imaging [3], photocatalysis [5], optical detection [6], photovoltaic conversion [7] and photoluminescence (PL) detection [8]. Current preparation strategies are mainly “bottom-up” [9] and “top-down” [10]. Unfortunately, GQDs obtained via current preparation strategies always contain complicated surficial chemistry micro-circumstance (such as surface/edge functional groups, lattice heteroatoms, surface/edge defects) [9,11]. The complicated surficial chemistry micro-circumstance of GQDs seriously hinders the further development of the theoretical researches and application explorations of GQDs [12]. Great efforts have been put into the interrelation between the surficial chemistry micro-circumstance and the basic properties of GQDs [13–28]. Yang et al. have demonstrated the interrelation between surficial chemistry micro-circumstance and optical properties of GQDs [29]. Huang et al. have demonstrated the important role of localized superacid microenvironment in the photocatalytic process of GQDs [30]. Huang et al. demonstrated the photocatalytic polymerization of amino acids into polypeptides and proteins using GQDs as a photocatalyst, in which the synthetic conditions required are only room temperature and aqueous conditions along with light irradiation [30]. However, most recent reports aimed to explore the relations between surficial chemistry micro-circumstance and specific properties of GQDs. The in-depth study of the synergistic effect is urgent and helpful for the application explorations of GQDs.

Homocysteine is an important biomarker for many key diseases. It is known that the low B12 levels can give rise to elevated homocysteine levels with an associated increased risk of many illnesses, including stroke and heart disease, without any typical B12 deficiency symptoms [31,32]. Moreover, the high levels of homocysteine also have been linked to a greater risk of Alzheimer's [33,34]. Thus, the development of efficient detection method for homocysteine is helpful for the clinical diagnosis. Strongin et al. described simple methods for detecting either homocysteine or cysteine in the visible spectral region with the high selectivity without using biochemical techniques or preparative separations. However, the reported HPLC post-column detection methods for biological thiols are still ineffective [35].

In this paper, we show the synergistic effect of surface catalysis and photoluminescence sensing of N-GQDs. Due to the localized superacid microenvironment of N-GQDs, the homocysteine cyclized into homocysteine thiolactone efficiently and selectively. Meanwhile, the aromatic N in N-GQDs adsorbs homocysteine thiolactone selectively. The following static quenching results in the outstanding selectivity and sensitivity PL quenching of N-GQDs with the presence of homocysteine. Thus, the N-GQDs can be used as a PL probe with high sensitivity and high selectivity. The detection limit is 5 × 10−11 M, which is much lower than most reported detection methods. We also demonstrate that N-GQDs are able to display a PL response to homocysteine in the living cells.

All chemicals were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China) and used as received without further purification. The typical preparation process of N-GQDs is as follows: 500 mg graphene oxide (GO) and 100 μL dimethylformamide (DMF) were dissolved in 10.0 mL deionized water. The mixture was transferred into a 15 mL Teflons-lined autoclave and heated at 140 °C for 48 h. The obtained reaction mixture was filtrated and dialyzed by 500 Da dialysis bag for 24 h. The yield of N-GQDs is 12.3 wt.% (calculated from p-phenylenediamine and triethylene glycol monomethyl ether).

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was captured using a Hitachi H-8100 electron microscope (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan) under 80 kV. The atomic force microscopy (AFM) was carried out using the Bruker Dimension Icon system. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were carried out on the PHI Quantera II system (Ulvac-PHI, INC, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded using a Bruker Vertex 70 infrared spectrometer. The ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) spectra were recorded using a UV-5800 spectrophotometer. The PL and photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectra were recorded using the PerkinElmer LS55 luminescence spectrometer (PerkinElmer Instruments, U.K.).

TEM and AFM images suggest the morphology of the obtained N-GQDs, as shown in Fig. 1 . Fig. 1a displays the TEM image of N-GQDs. The homogeneous dots with 0.5–5.5 nm lateral size can be observed. The distribution histogram of lateral size for the obtained N-GQDs is shown in Fig. 1b, and the average lateral size is 3 nm. The full width at half maximum (FWHM) is 2 nm, which further demonstrate the uniform lateral size of these N-GQDs. The obvious lattice structure can be observed in the high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image (Fig. 1c), which indicates the excellent crystallinity of these obtained N-GQDs. The interlayer spacing of 0.24 nm corresponds to the (1120) lattice fringe of graphene [36]. Moreover, no obvious diffraction peak can be observed in the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of N-GQDs (Fig. S1), which indicates the monolayer structure of most obtained N-GQDs [12]. Moreover, the XRD results did not show the amorphous signal of N-GQDs [12]. Fig. 1d displays a representative AFM image of highly-dispersed N-GQDs on a 300 nm SiO2/Si substrate. The thicknesses of the N-GQDs are approximately 0.4–1.4 nm, which means N-GQDs consisting of 1–3 layers [37]. Raman spectra of N-GQDs were displayed in Fig. 1f. The defect-related D and G bands can be observed at 1350 and 1580 cm−1, respectively [38]. The peak intensity ratio of D and G bands (ID/IG) is 0.41.

Previous reports have demonstrated the shearing and in situ N doping mechanism of GO under the hydrothermal environment with the presence of DMF [12]. XPS results (Fig. 2 ) further clarified the surface chemical structure of the obtained N-GQDs. Fig. 2a displays the XPS survey spectrum of N-GQDs. The peak located at ca. 284.2 eV, ca. 532 eV and ca. 399 eV can be attributed to the C 1s, O 1s and N 1s peak of N-GQDs, respectively [39]. Due to the low usage amount of DMF in the preparation process, the N content of N-GQDs is 3.1 at.% [40]. By contrast, the O content of N-GQDs is 16.5 at.%. Fig. 2b displays the well-fitted C 1s spectrum of N-GQDs. The peaks located at 284.5, 286.2, 288.2, and 289.6 eV can be attributed to the CC/CC, CO/CN, CO, and COOH groups, respectively [41]. The O 1s spectrum (Fig. 2c) indicates the diversified oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of N-GQDs, including CO (532.8 eV) and CO (531.2 eV) [42]. By contrast, most N atoms exist in the form of aromatic N (398.7 eV, Fig. 2d) [43]. FT-IR result (Fig. S2) further conformed above the results, and the surface chemical structure of the obtained N-GQDs is shown in the inset of Fig. 2a.

UV–vis, PL, and PLE spectra were carried out for the representation for the optical properties of N-GQDs. Fig. 3 a displays the UV–vis spectrum of N-GQDs aqueous solution. The obvious two absorption peaks at 280 and 360 nm can be observed. The peak at 280 nm can be attributed to π-π* transition of the sp2 domains [44]. The peak located at 360 nm can be attributed to the n-π* transition (or absorption by σ bonds and single aromatic rings) of aromatic N in N-GQDs [29,45]. The strong signal of the n-π* transition (or π-π* transitions) model indicates the N-GQDs have outstanding PL performance [37,46]. PL and PLE spectra (Fig. 3b) suggest the optimal excitation wavelength and an emission wavelength of N-GQDs are 342 and 478 nm, respectively. The quantum yield (φ) of N-GQDs is 0.82, which is much higher than most reported GQDs, carbon dots, and polymer dots [3,44,47–57]. This can be due to the efficient n-π* transition process of aromatic N in N-GQDs [37]. The PL decay of N-GQDs is measured by a time-correlated single-photon counting technique and fitted well with a biexponential decay. The lifetime (τ) is dominated by a long decay component of 6.5 ns (92%) plus a small contribution from the short decay of 1.5 ns (8%); the weighted-average lifetime is approximately 6.1 ns (Fig. 3c). Radiative rates of N-GQDs [58] (κr, κr = φ/τ) is 1.34 × 108 s−1. Moreover, the N-GQDs also show excellent stability under different pH, visible light irradiation and ultraviolet irradiation (Figs S3–S5). This can be due to the stable surface chemical structure of N-GQDs [59].

It is interesting that the PL of N-GQDs is quenched with the presence of homocysteine. As shown in Fig. 3d, with the increased concentration of homocysteine (0−1 × 10−6 M), the PL intensity of N-GQDs dramatically reduced. The PL intensity of N-GQDs follows the equation as follows [60]:
F0/F = 1 + K[Hom]
where [Hom] is the homocysteine concentration, and F0 and F are PL intensities of N-GQDs at 478 nm in the absence and presence of homocysteine, respectively. The PL intensity of N-GQDs shows good linear dependence with [Hom] in the range of 5 × 10−11–5 × 10−8 M (Fig. 3e). The detection limit is 5 × 10−11 M at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3 (estimated by extrapolating the Stern–Volmer plot), which is much lower than most reported detection methods (Table 1 ) [61–66]. Besides, the N-GQDs show outstanding selectivity with the presence of common ions (Na+, K+, Mg2+, Al3+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Ag+, Pb2+, Hg2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, SO4 2- and Cl-, Fig. 3f) and amino acids (lysine, threonine, phenylalanine, cysteine, valine, methionine, tryptophan, glutamine, proline, Fig. 3g). No obvious change of PL intensity can be observed with the presence of common ions or amino acids. Moreover, the N-GQDs show high selectivity for homocysteine. N-GQDs show on obvious PL quenching with other biothiols (such as glutathione, captopril, and coenzyme A, as shown in Fig. S6).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synergistic effect of surface catalysis and photoluminescence sensing for N-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The outstanding selectivity and sensitivity of N-GQDs suggest the possible unique mechanism in the detection process. Control experiments and density functional theory (DFT) calculations were carried out for the in-depth mechanism study. Previous reports have demonstrated the localized superacid microenvironment of GQDs [30]. Moreover, it is well known that the cyclization reaction of homocysteine can be catalyzed in the acid environment (Fig. 4 a) [37]. Homocysteine thiolactone will be obtained after the cyclization reaction of homocysteine. Thus, the surface catalytic cyclization reaction of homocysteine will exist at the localized superacid microenvironment of N-GQDs.

DFT calculation was carried out for evaluation of the Gibbs free energy (ΔG) of surface catalytic cyclization reaction for the homocysteine thiolactone. As shown in Fig. 4b, the ΔG decreases with the increased oxygen-containing functional group content of GQDs. The ΔG is −55.3, −293.5, −334.5, −395.6, −403.6 and −411.8 kJ mol−1 when the oxygen-containing functional groups content of N-GQDs is 0, 3, 6, 9, 12 and 15 at.%, respectively, which indicates the significant surface catalytic at the localized superacid microenvironment of N-GQDs. N-GQDs (N-GQDs-1, N-GQDs-2, N-GQDs-3, and N-GQDs-4) with different oxygen-containing functional groups content and the same N content (2.8–3.2 at.%) are prepared for further demonstrating above process. The oxygen-containing functional groups content of N-GQDs, N-GQDs-1, N-GQDs-2, N-GQDs-3, and N-GQDs-4 is 16.5, 11.3, 8.98, 4.78, and 1.68 at.%, respectively. Typically, 5 mL, 1 mM homocysteine aqueous solution and 5 mL, 1 mg mL−1 N-GQDs aqueous solution are mixed and stirred for 10 min under 25 °C. The content of homocysteine thiolactone is determined by a liquid chromatogram. As shown in Fig. 4c, the content of homocysteine thiolactone is 0.38, 0.34, 0.31, 0.23, and 0.06 mM when the homocysteine aqueous solution mixed with N-GQDs, N-GQDs-1, N-GQDs-2, N-GQDs-3, and N-GQDs-4 aqueous solution, respectively. The remarkable decreased homocysteine thiolactone content with the decreased oxygen-containing functional groups content of N-GQDs demonstrate the surface catalytic cyclization reaction of homocysteine at the localized superacid microenvironment of N-GQDs. On the other hand, due to the shorter carbon-carbon skeletal chain of cysteine (Fig. 4a), the cyclic structure cannot form at the localized superacid microenvironment of N-GQDs. Thus, the N-GQDs show high selectivity for homocysteine.

Further, DFT calculations and control experiments demonstrated the selective chemical adsorption and static quenching process between N-GQDs and homocysteine thiolactone. The ΔG is −91.8, 16.4, and 2.6 kJ mol−1 when the N atoms in N-GQDs are aromatic N, NH2 and CNHC, respectively (Fig. 4d). The ΔG ＞ 0 when the N atoms in N-GQDs are -NH2, and CNHC. In contrast, the ΔG ＜ 0 when the N atoms in N-GQDs are aromatic N. Moreover, the ΔG ＞ 0 when N-GQDs adsorb other amino acids (include lysine, threonine, phenylalanine, cysteine, homocysteine, valine, methionine, tryptophan, glutamine and proline, Fig. 4e). This can be due to the strong interaction between sulfur contained five-membered ring structure of homocysteine thiolactone and aromatic N. This indicates the effective selective chemical adsorption of aromatic N for the homocysteine thiolactone. NH2-GQDs and NH-GQDs with the same oxygen-containing functional group contents (15.5–16.3 at.%) and N content are prepared for further demonstrating the above process. The PL intensity of NH2-GQDs and NH-GQDs show no obvious change with the presence of homocysteine (Fig. 4f). Lifetime measurements were carried out for further evidence of the quenching mechanism. In static quenching, the PL lifetime does not change (τ0/τ = 1, where τ0 and τ are the PL lifetime of N-GQDs in the absence and presence of homocysteine, respectively) [37]. No obvious change of τ0/τ can be observed under different homocysteine concentrations (Fig. 4f).

Thus, due to the localized superacid microenvironment of N-GQDs, the homocysteine cyclized into homocysteine thiolactone efficiently and selectively. Meanwhile, the aromatic N in N-GQDs adsorbs homocysteine thiolactone selectively. The following static quenching results in the outstanding selectivity and sensitivity PL quenching of N-GQDs with the presence of homocysteine.

Finally, we show the in vitro detection of homocysteine by N-GQDs. The cytotoxicity of N-GQDs is evaluated by rat adipose-derived stem cells (rADSCs cells). No obvious reduction in cell viability of rADSCs cells (Fig. 5 a) can be observed after N-GQDs treatment with different concentrations (0−600 μg mL−1). The viability of rADSCs cells is 99.7%, 99.3%, 99.2%, 98.9%, 98.9%, 97.7%, 98.2%, 98.3%, 97.7%, 96.4%, 97.5%, 96.8% and 96.1% after 24 h when the N-GQDs concentration is 0, 50, 100, 150, 200, 250, 300, 350, 400, 450, 500, 550 and 600 μg mL−1. rADSCs cells treated with N-GQDs emitted strong cyan PL (Fig. 4b) around their nuclei. However, when homocysteine is introduced, the resulting quenched PL (Fig. 6c) demonstrate that N-GQDs can display a PL response to homocysteine in the living cells.

In summary, we demonstrate the selectivity and sensitivity PL detection of homocysteine based on the synergistic effect of surface catalysis and photoluminescence sensing for N-GQDs, which attributes to the localized superacid microenvironment of N-GQDs, the homocysteine cyclized into homocysteine thiolactone efficiently and selectively. Meanwhile, the aromatic N in N-GQDs adsorbs homocysteine thiolactone selectively. The following static quenching results in the outstanding selectivity and sensitivity PL quenching of N-GQDs with the presence of homocysteine. The detection limit is 5 × 10−11 M, which is much lower than most reported detection methods. We also demonstrate that N-GQDs can display a PL response to homocysteine in the living cells. Our work is helpful for the in-depth understanding of the surficial chemistry micro-circumstance of GQDs and the design of selectivity and sensitivity sensors based on GQDs.
The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

Z. W. and G. W. conceived the project. Z. W., G. W., Y. Z., X. F. and Y. Z. designed and performed the experiments. W. Z., M. Z., T. W., Z. L. and S. T. performed the data analysis. Z. W., G. W., Y. Z., Q. G. and D. C. contributed to the discussion. Z. W., Z. L. and S. T. and G. W. prepared the figures. Z. W., Y. Z., G. W., T. W. and D. C. co-wrote the paper. All authors discussed the results and commented on the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support provided by the Open Fund of the State Key Laboratory on Integrated Optoelectronics under Grant (No. IOSKL2017KF06), Natural Science Foundation of Ningbo under Grant (No. 2018A6100960), National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant (Nos. 11704204 and 61604084), and K. C. Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.synthmet.2020.116432.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Zhu, S. W. Yang, G. Wang, R. W. Mo, P. He, J. Sun, Z. F. Di, Z. H. Kang, N. Y. Yuan, J. N. Ding, G. Q. Ding, X. M. Xie, J. Mater. Chem. B. 3 (2015) 6871.
Y. Li, Y. Zhao, H. H. Cheng, Y. Hu, G. Q. Shi, L. M. Dai, L. T. Qu, J. Am. Chem. Soc. 134 (2012) 15.
